expanded_description: A median of a triangle is a segment from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. Every triangle has three medians, and they are concurrent at a single point called the centroid. The centroid is the balance point or center of mass of the triangle. Its defining property is that it divides each median into two segments in a 2:1 ratio—the distance from the vertex to the centroid is twice the distance from the centroid to the midpoint of the opposite side. The centroid is always inside the triangle.

core_concept.principle: A median connects a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. The three medians of a triangle intersect at the centroid $G$. The centroid divides each median in a 2:1 ratio: the segment from the vertex to $G$ is twice as long as the segment from $G$ to the midpoint of the opposite side. Symbolically, if $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{BC}$, then $AG : GM = 2 : 1$, or $AG = 2(GM)$ and $GM = \frac{1}{3}AM$. The centroid is always inside the triangle and is the triangle's center of mass.
core_concept.explanation: Think of the centroid as the triangle's balancing point. If you cut a triangle out of cardboard, you can balance it on a pencil tip placed at the centroid. Each median is like a line of partial balance—the triangle balances along it if you ignore the third vertex. The intersection of all three medians is the unique point where the triangle balances in every direction. The 2:1 ratio is what makes this work: the centroid is closer to the side than to the opposite vertex because the side's mass pulls the balance point toward it. In coordinates, the centroid is simply the average of the three vertex coordinates—an elegant formula that makes it the easiest triangle center to compute.

image.alt: A triangle with all three medians drawn, intersecting at the centroid G. Each median labeled with the 2:1 ratio—segment from vertex to G marked 2x, from G to midpoint marked x. A smaller diagram shows the triangle balancing on a pencil tip at G.
image.caption: The centroid G is the intersection of the medians. It divides each median in a 2:1 ratio.

sections.introduction: A median is a segment joining a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. Unlike perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors, medians are not generally perpendicular to the sides and do not generally bisect the angles. Each median divides the triangle into two smaller triangles of equal area.

introduction_image.alt: A triangle with one median drawn from vertex A to midpoint M of side BC. Two smaller triangles ABM and ACM shaded differently with equal area labels.
introduction_image.caption: A median connects a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. It divides the triangle into two equal-area triangles.

key_concept.definition: A median of a triangle is a segment from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. Every triangle has three medians. If $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{BC}$, then $\overline{AM}$ is a median. Key property: A median divides the triangle into two triangles of equal area. Since $\triangle ABM$ and $\triangle ACM$ share the same altitude from $A$ and have equal bases $\overline{BM}$ and $\overline{CM}$, their areas are equal: each is half the area of the original triangle.
key_concept.context: The equal-area property of a median is the key to proving the concurrency of medians and the 2:1 ratio at the centroid. It also has practical applications: the median is the line along which the triangle balances if considered as a physical object with mass concentrated along that line.

image.alt: Triangle with median AM. Midpoint M marked on BC. Triangles ABM and ACM shaded, with equal area labels. Bases BM and CM marked congruent.
image.caption: A median divides the triangle into two equal-area triangles.

process_flow.description: To construct a median, find the midpoint of a side and draw the segment from the opposite vertex to that midpoint.
process_flow.mechanism: Use the perpendicular bisector construction to locate the midpoint of a side. Then use the straightedge to draw the segment from the opposite vertex to that midpoint. No new construction technique is needed.
process_flow.result: The median from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.
applications: To construct median from $A$ to $\overline{BC}$: construct the perpendicular bisector of $\overline{BC}$ to find its midpoint $M$, then draw $\overline{AM}$.

image.alt: Construction of a median: perpendicular bisector of BC locates midpoint M, then segment AM is drawn.
image.caption: Step 1: Construct the perpendicular bisector of a side to find its midpoint, then draw the median.

process_flow.description: Recognize that the median divides the triangle into two triangles of equal area.
process_flow.mechanism: The two resulting triangles share the same height (the perpendicular distance from the vertex to the opposite side). Their bases are congruent because the midpoint divides the side into two equal segments. Since area = $\frac{1}{2}(\text{base})(\text{height})$, equal bases and equal heights give equal areas.
applications: If the area of $\triangle ABC$ is 60 square units and $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{BC}$, then the area of $\triangle ABM$ is 30 and the area of $\triangle ACM$ is 30.

image.alt: Triangle with median. Heights from A to BC shown. Bases BM and CM marked equal. Area formula displayed.
image.caption: Step 2: A median creates two triangles with equal bases and the same height, hence equal areas.

image.alt: Complete section overview: median definition, construction, and equal-area property.
image.caption: Medians: definition, construction, and the equal-area property.

sections.introduction: The three medians of a triangle are concurrent. Their intersection is called the centroid, denoted $G$. The centroid is the center of mass of the triangle and has the remarkable property that it divides each median in a 2:1 ratio, with the longer segment at the vertex end.

introduction_image.alt: A triangle with all three medians drawn, intersecting at centroid G. The 2:1 ratio marked on each median. A physical balancing demonstration showing the triangle supported at G.
introduction_image.caption: The centroid G: intersection of medians, center of mass, divides each median 2:1.

key_concept.definition: The centroid $G$ of a triangle is the point of concurrency of the three medians. The centroid divides each median into two segments with lengths in the ratio $2:1$, with the longer segment adjacent to the vertex. If $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{BC}$, then $AG : GM = 2 : 1$. Equivalently, $AG = \frac{2}{3}AM$ and $GM = \frac{1}{3}AM$. The centroid is always located inside the triangle. In coordinates, if the vertices are $A(x_1, y_1)$, $B(x_2, y_2)$, $C(x_3, y_3)$, then the centroid is $G\left(\frac{x_1+x_2+x_3}{3}, \frac{y_1+y_2+y_3}{3}\right)$.
key_concept.context: The 2:1 ratio is proved by connecting the midpoints of two sides. The segment joining the midpoints is parallel to the third side and half its length. Using similar triangles formed by the intersection of two medians, the 2:1 ratio emerges. This is one of the most elegant proofs in geometry.

image.alt: Triangle with medians intersecting at G. The 2:1 ratio marked. The coordinate formula displayed. The triangle's midsegment shown connecting midpoints, parallel to the third side.
image.caption: Centroid: divides each median 2:1. Coordinates are the average of the vertices.

process_flow.description: To locate the centroid, construct the medians from any two vertices. Their intersection is the centroid.
process_flow.mechanism: Find the midpoint of $\overline{BC}$ (call it $M$) and draw median $\overline{AM}$. Find the midpoint of $\overline{AC}$ (call it $N$) and draw median $\overline{BN}$. The intersection of $\overline{AM}$ and $\overline{BN}$ is the centroid $G$. The third median from $C$ will also pass through $G$. In coordinate geometry, the centroid is simply the average of the vertex coordinates.
process_flow.result: The centroid $G$ located as the intersection of two medians.

image.alt: Triangle with two medians drawn, intersecting at G. Midpoints marked.
image.caption: Step 1: Construct two medians. Their intersection is the centroid G.

process_flow.description: The centroid divides each median so that the distance from vertex to centroid is twice the distance from centroid to the midpoint of the opposite side.
process_flow.mechanism: If $AG = 2(GM)$, then $GM = \frac{1}{3}AM$ and $AG = \frac{2}{3}AM$. This relationship allows finding the centroid's location along any median. Given the length of a median, the centroid is located two-thirds of the way from the vertex to the midpoint.
process_flow.result: The segment lengths along any median, determined by the 2:1 ratio.
applications: If $G$ is 6 units from vertex $A$ along median $AM$, then $AM = \frac{3}{2}(6) = 9$.

image.alt: Median with segments labeled: AG = 2x, GM = x. Full median AM = 3x.
image.caption: Step 2: The centroid divides each median in a 2:1 ratio (vertex to centroid : centroid to midpoint).

classifications.value: The centroid divides each median in the ratio 2:1, with the longer segment at the vertex. This is the defining property of the centroid.
characteristics: The same ratio applies to all three medians.
This property is unique among triangle centers.
classifications.behavior: Given any median and the location of the centroid, the 2:1 ratio determines all segment lengths along that median.

examples.explanation: Median $\overline{AM}$ has length 15. $G$ is the centroid. $AG = \frac{2}{3}(15) = 10$. $GM = \frac{1}{3}(15) = 5$. Check: $10 + 5 = 15$ and $10:5 = 2:1$.

image.alt: Median AM with G dividing it 2:1. AG=10, GM=5, AM=15.
image.caption: 2:1 ratio: AG = 2·GM, so AG = 2/3 of the median.

classifications.value: The centroid coordinates are the arithmetic mean of the vertex coordinates: $G = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2+x_3}{3}, \frac{y_1+y_2+y_3}{3}\right)$.
characteristics: This formula holds for any triangle.
It is derived from the 2:1 ratio property.
The centroid is the only triangle center with such a simple coordinate formula.
This makes the centroid the easiest center to compute.
classifications.behavior: Averaging the x-coordinates gives the x-coordinate of the centroid. Averaging the y-coordinates gives the y-coordinate.

image.alt: Triangle on coordinate plane with vertices labeled. Centroid calculated and plotted at (4,2).
image.caption: Centroid = average of vertex coordinates.

image.alt: Complete section overview: centroid definition, 2:1 ratio, coordinate formula, and center of mass property.
image.caption: The Centroid: concurrency of medians, 2:1 ratio, and coordinate formula.

sections.introduction: The concurrency of the medians and the 2:1 ratio are proved using coordinate geometry or similar triangles. Once established, these properties become powerful tools for solving problems involving segment lengths along medians and for coordinate calculations.

introduction_image.alt: Triangle with two medians intersecting. Midsegment connecting midpoints shown parallel to the third side. Similar triangles highlighted.
introduction_image.caption: Proof strategy: connect midpoints to create similar triangles and establish the 2:1 ratio.

key_concept.definition: Coordinate Proof of Concurrency: Place the triangle with vertices $A(0,0)$, $B(2a,0)$, $C(2b,2c)$. The midpoints are $M(a+b, c)$ of $\overline{BC}$, $N(b, c)$ of $\overline{AC}$, and $P(a, 0)$ of $\overline{AB}$. The medians $\overline{AM}$ and $\overline{BN}$ intersect at $G\left(\frac{2a+2b}{3}, \frac{2c}{3}\right)$. This point lies two-thirds of the way from each vertex to the opposite midpoint, establishing both the 2:1 ratio and concurrency. The third median $\overline{CP}$ also passes through $G$. In simplified form, $G = \left(\frac{0+2a+2b}{3}, \frac{0+0+2c}{3}\right)$, the average of the vertex coordinates.
key_concept.context: This proof demonstrates the power of coordinate geometry. By strategically placing the triangle, the algebra becomes manageable, and the result is both the 2:1 ratio and the coordinate formula.

image.alt: Triangle placed on coordinate plane with vertices as described. Medians drawn, intersection G calculated. Steps of the algebra shown.
image.caption: Coordinate proof: strategic placement yields both the 2:1 ratio and the centroid formula.

worked_examples.title: Centroid Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) In $\triangle ABC$, median $\overline{AM}$ has length 15. $G$ is the centroid. Find $AG$ and $GM$.
2) Find the centroid of the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(9,0)$, $(3,6)$.
3) In $\triangle PQR$, $G$ is the centroid. $PG = 8$. Find the length of the entire median from $P$.
4) Prove that the medians of a triangle are concurrent using coordinates.

step_by_step.step: 3) Find median length from centroid distance
step_by_step.explanation: $PG = \frac{2}{3}$ of the median from $P$. Let the median be $\overline{PM}$ where $M$ is the midpoint of the opposite side. $PG = \frac{2}{3}PM$, so $8 = \frac{2}{3}PM$. $PM = \frac{3}{2}(8) = 12$.

Place $A(0,0)$, $B(2a,0)$, $C(2b,2c)$. Midpoints: $M(a+b,c)$ of $\overline{BC}$, $N(b,c)$ of $\overline{AC}$, $P(a,0)$ of $\overline{AB}$. Intersection of medians $\overline{AM}$ and $\overline{BN}$ is $G\left(\frac{2a+2b}{3}, \frac{2c}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{0+2a+2b}{3}, \frac{0+0+2c}{3}\right)$. This is the average of the vertex coordinates, and it lies on all three medians, proving concurrency and the 2:1 ratio.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Median with AG=10, GM=5. (2) Triangle with centroid (4,2). (3) Median with PG=8, PM=12. (4) Coordinate proof diagram with vertices and medians.
image.caption: Centroid problems: 2:1 ratio, coordinate formula, and concurrency proof.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) $AG = 10$, $GM = 5$.
2) $G(4, 2)$.
3) Median from $P$ has length 12.
4) See step-by-step for the coordinate proof.
worked_examples.concept_applied: 2:1 ratio, centroid coordinate formula, and coordinate proof of concurrency.

sections.introduction: Mastery of centroid concepts requires practice applying the 2:1 ratio, computing centroid coordinates, and understanding the equal-area property of medians. Work through these problems to build fluency.

worked_examples.title: Centroid Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) In $\triangle ABC$, the median from $A$ has length 18. $G$ is the centroid. Find $AG$.
2) Find the centroid of the triangle with vertices $(2,1)$, $(8,1)$, $(5,7)$.
3) In $\triangle XYZ$, the centroid $G$ lies on median $\overline{XM}$. $XG = 6$. Find $XM$ and $GM$.
4) True or False: The centroid of a triangle can be outside the triangle.

step_by_step.explanation: False. The centroid is the intersection of medians, which are segments from vertices to midpoints of opposite sides. These segments all lie inside the triangle, so their intersection must also lie inside. The centroid is always inside the triangle, for all triangle types.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Median with AG=12. (2) Triangle with centroid (5,3). (3) Median with XG=6, XM=9. (4) Diagram showing centroid always inside.
image.caption: Centroid practice: 2:1 ratio, coordinate formula, and location property.

worked_examples.concept_applied: 2:1 ratio, centroid coordinate formula, and the always-inside property of the centroid.

summary: The medians of a triangle are concurrent at the centroid. Key points: 1) A median joins a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. It divides the triangle into two equal-area triangles. 2) The centroid $G$ is the intersection of the three medians. It is always inside the triangle. 3) $G$ divides each median in a 2:1 ratio: $AG = \frac{2}{3}AM$, $GM = \frac{1}{3}AM$. 4) In coordinates, $G = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2+x_3}{3}, \frac{y_1+y_2+y_3}{3}\right)$—the average of the vertex coordinates. 5) The centroid is the center of mass (balancing point) of the triangle. 6) The centroid is one of the four major triangle centers, alongside the circumcenter, incenter, and orthocenter.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Centroid' in center. Branches: Medians (vertex to midpoint, equal-area property), Concurrency (intersection of three medians), 2:1 Ratio (vertex to G = 2 × G to midpoint), Coordinate Formula (average of vertices), Center of Mass (balancing point), Location (always inside).
review_questions_image.caption: Medians and Centroids Review: the concurrency, 2:1 ratio, and coordinate formula.
